ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bánh hỏi thịt nướng; closest defensible anchor is the generic noodle dish class. Exact Vietnamese dish term is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pork dishes) is the closest defensible category. No specific bánh hỏi composite dish code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of bánh hỏi thịt nướng as served in a Vietnamese restaurant or hawker stall in Singapore typically weighs approximately 250 g, comprising a bundle of thin rice vermicelli sheets topped with sliced grilled pork, scallion oil, and crushed prawn.

nutrient_source.title: Super Thin Vermicelli With Grilled Pork (Bánh Hỏi Thịt Nướng Heo) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010625). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are not reported; set to -1. Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB — this is a known data anomaly for a dish that contains rice vermicelli; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice noodles / vermicelli analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: HPB reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g for this dish, which is a known data anomaly (bánh hỏi is a rice starch product). Because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g, the glycemic load per serving calculates to 0.0. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli / rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008, Table entry for rice noodles, GI ~53, low category). This estimate is documented here for completeness; the GL per serving is 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g is driven by fish sauce marinade and seasoning, and a standard 250 g serving would deliver approximately 2625 mg sodium, well above the 2000 mg daily recommended limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: HPB reports zero carbohydrate, but bánh hỏi is a rice starch product; actual glycaemic impact may be underestimated; high sodium is an additional metabolic risk factor
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat the reported zero carbohydrate with caution — bánh hỏi rice vermicelli sheets are a starch-based food and would contribute to postprandial glucose; request plain preparation without sweet dipping sauces and monitor blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100 g and cholesterol 89 mg/100 g combined with red meat (pork) and unknown saturated fat content pose significant cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to very high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g), cholesterol (89 mg/100 g), and the saturated fat contribution of grilled pork; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to no more than 100 g and paired with high-potassium vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100 g exceeds the RA threshold of 400 mg/100 g; pork contains arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g worsens tissue swelling, and pork-derived arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifies inflammatory signalling; consider substituting with grilled salmon or chicken with reduced-sodium seasoning.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Hỏi Thịt Nướng Heo is a Vietnamese dish consisting of super-thin woven rice vermicelli sheets (bánh hỏi) served with sliced grilled pork (thịt nướng heo), scallion oil, and crushed prawn. The rice vermicelli is pressed into fine, tightly woven bundles with a firm yet delicate texture that absorbs the savoury scallion oil dressing. The grilled pork is typically marinated in fish sauce, lemongrass, garlic, and sugar before being charcoal-grilled, producing a caramelised, smoky exterior. The dish is commonly served with fresh herbs, lettuce, and a fish-sauce-based dipping sauce (nước chấm). It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Vietnamese cuisine and is found in Vietnamese restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database reports 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, and 10.5 g fat per 100 g, with sodium at a very high 1050 mg/100 g due to fish sauce marinade and seasoning. Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB, which is a known data anomaly — bánh hỏi is a rice starch product and would contribute carbohydrate in a complete dish analysis. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and cholesterol content, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the starch component and high sodium.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010625, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. The exact dish entry is present in HPB. Confidence is high for energy, protein, fat, sodium, and micronutrients as reported. The carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g is flagged as a known data anomaly but is reproduced faithfully from the approved source.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore Foodo for this dish entry. This is a known data anomaly — bánh hỏi is a rice starch product and would be expected to contribute carbohydrate. The HPB value is reproduced faithfully as the approved source value; however, users should be aware that actual carbohydrate content may be higher. Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is plausible if the marinade sugar is minimal and the dish is analysed without sweet dipping sauce — confirmed zero per HPB. Dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is plausible for a refined rice vermicelli and lean pork dish with no vegetable components in the analysed portion — confirmed zero per HPB. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): saturated_fat_g set to -1: HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish entry; detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo database for G00010625 as of 2026. monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: not reported by HPB for this entry. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: not reported by HPB for this entry. trans_fat_g set to -1: not reported by HPB for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported by HPB for this dish entry as of 2026. GI NOTE: GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli from the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per serving is 0.0 based on HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g, which is the approved source value. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: This dish is Vietnamese in origin but served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for English, Vietnamese, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements. Malay name notes that pork is not halal — the dish is not suitable for Muslim consumers.
